LinkedIn and 3rd parties use essential and non-essential cookies to provide, secure, analyze and improve our Services, and to show you relevant ads (including professional and job ads) on and off LinkedIn. Learn more in our Cookie Policy.

Select Accept to consent or Reject to decline non-essential cookies for this use. You can update your choices at any time in your settings.

Tor-Bjorn Fjellner’s Post

Tor-Bjorn Fjellner
1y Edited
  • Report this post

Just filled out an impossible form to apply for a job Here's what makes that form impossible: the last question reads "Salary expecations*". 1. The star means that I must answer this question. 2. So I want to say "It depends", an error message pops up: "You must respond with a decimal value that is larger than 0.0". 3. What salary is expressed in only numbers, without a unit? Are we talking Euros, Turkish Lira, or USD? Is this before taxes, or net? Is this per hour, day worked, calendar day, week, month, or year? I also understand that this is an attempt at filtering for people with low expectations. But maybe not too low. Just how much self-esteem in a candidate is best for the employer? I mean, they should be cheap, right? But also alive enough to perform well and earn the employer Millions. (See, I also know how to omit currency symbols and periodicity :D ) Worst of all? This specific question seems to be included in some library of standard questions in LinkedIn Easy Apply, so I have to answer it time and again. Well, at least I'm forced to enter any number. Oh, and soon enough, EU legislation will make this question illegal. Instead, employers will be obliged to indicate suggested salary ranges whenever they're publishing a vacancy. That's it. Now I have complained enough for today. Wish me good luck with this application! The job description feels like a really good match for me. PS! It would be interesting to know how you recruiters look at this type of question.

305 200 Comments
Like Comment
Guy Allen 1y
  • Report this comment

I don't have a problem with it as a required question on the condition of the following: A) the salary budget of the role is specified on the advert B) the field to enter this is free-text, allowing for some flexibility to provide a salary range or some additional detail Otherwise I agree, it makes it impossible. It's ridiculous to be held to a salary expectation figure based upon reading an advert. There will inevitably be new information that comes to light or changes in circumstances (at home, in current role, with other opportunities being explored) that changes your expectations, hence why I always prefer to provide a salary range to my clients to consider a candidate against as opposed to a singular figure.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction 2 Reactions
Louis Corallo 1y
  • Report this comment

I hate this question. In Vietnam where I’m based, I avoid it like the plague. They want salary expectations and I know they already have a number in mind so why not just list it and cut out the nonsense? If my number is too high then they will say no instead of figuring out how and why we should work together. If my number is modest then I will end up looking like I either don’t value myself or I’m just trying to place an interview.

Like
Reply
2 Reactions 3 Reactions
Justin Boucher, MBA 1y
  • Report this comment

I skipped those when I was job hunting. If a company is not going to understand a measure of flexibility when it comes to compensation then they should just post what they're offering and see how many applicants apply for that rate. It comes across as a complete trap when a company states that compensation is based on experience in the posting but then forces applicants to set a specific value. There are far too many variables in a total compensation package to lock down a specific value at first contact.

Like
Reply
2 Reactions 3 Reactions
Sara Nelson 1y
  • Report this comment

In the US, I'm lucky(?) enough to be in one of the few States that require employers post compensation amounts. Of course that only applies if the business is actively targeting people in my State...otherwise, if it's a position that applies to all of the US (no defined States), they don't have to include it. Anyways, applied for a role the other day that had a posted range of $45k to $75k per year. And then on the application, they still asked what my expected compensation was. It's like...here's a thought (posed to them). Instead of including a range you clearly aren't going to pay for this role, why not narrow the focus down to $45k and call it a day? Win/Win all around.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction 2 Reactions
Zahra K. 1y
  • Report this comment

I hated this question when I was an employee, and now when I am an employer, I still do not understand what this question's point is. Employers will still not give you what you want, it is still good if they give you what you deserve. But If they want to know your POV in self-estimation, then still not good. Probably HR agencies or hr Dep put this ridiculous illegal question. As an Employer, I will consider your character, skill, and experience. And as a Employee, this question matters to you more than to me.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction 2 Reactions
Michael T. 1y
  • Report this comment

I often pick the lower end of a salary range not because I don't feel I'm worth it or lack the confidence, but because how much a company is willing to pay me isn't the main reason I'm interested - I'm there because it's a company I want to work for. Perhaps it's the wrong outlook, but I would be turned off by a potential employer or employee whose salary is the only thing they care about. I do wish this question didn't exist on applications, since they're going to offer the salary range they advertised anyway.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction 2 Reactions
Frank Saxton 1y
  • Report this comment

Any company making a hiring decision based on compensation alone is not a company you want to be working at. They will have someone else sitting in your chair tomorrow the second a lower bidder comes along. Respect yourself even if an employer disrespects you.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction 2 Reactions
Nichole R. 1y
  • Report this comment

I had thought requesting salary desires was outlawed in the US a few years ago. Apparently not. We get this question all the time like you. However, I don’t see it as a means for employers to gauge a candidate’s self esteem by requesting low. It’s more likely to see if they’re asking too much or are within range. As for having to repeatedly answer it, that’s likely bc the questions are built into any and all HR-related software. Since we enter apps that send us through one or multiple similar apps, which makes it a UX/UI/developer issue. No one thinks through the whole process.

Like

  • Aidil Fahmi
    8mo
    • Report this post

    HR: “So your expected salary is RM3,800?” Candidate: “Yes, because the job post mentioned the range RM3,500–RM4,000.” HR: “Why do you feel you deserve RM3,800?” Candidate: “With my skills and experience, I believe that figure is fair.” HR: “That’s actually higher than what we usually offer. Most of the time, we don’t go beyond the midpoint of the range. The best we could possibly do is RM3,750, and that’s only for an exceptional hire.” Candidate: “Do I meet the requirements listed for this role?” HR: “Yes, you do.” Candidate: “Does my background and experience match the position?” HR: “Yes, but…” Candidate: “Then why did you shortlist me for the interview?” HR: “Because you have the skills and qualities we’re looking for.” Candidate: “So, what makes me not worth RM3,800?” HR: … ✨ Takeaway: Know your worth and be confident in salary discussions. If you’ve done your research and understand your value, it’s easier to negotiate without second-guessing yourself. ✅ Want to prepare for your next negotiation? Use our free Salary Checker: https://lnkd.in/g6_Aj2m4 to research the right range before applying Browse 10,000+ jobs (WFH, Part-time, Fresh Grad & more) here: https://lnkd.in/g3Nf4xGd

  • Anil Aphale
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Jack Dowden and the organisation missed out on a great candidate because no one could be bothered to pick up the telephone and have a conversation with you. The Natural assumption in these case from a candidate is that your number was too high - BUT - The organisation should have at least had a discussion with you - Their may have been benefits that meant a lower base was acceptable etc. Their loss - onwards and upwards

    Jack Dowden

    Customer Success I Strengthening Client Partnerships

    8mo

    Yesterday, I received a polite, yet frustrating, response to a recent job application, that I was not selected to progress because my salary expectations could not be met. This situation perfectly illustrates a major inefficiency in a current hiring trend. If a salary range had been clearly included in the original job advertisement, I would have immediately self-selected out of the process. Hiding this critical information is a needless waste of time and resources for everyone involved, the candidate, the recruiter, and the hiring team. It prevents the company from focusing its valuable efforts on candidates who are truly aligned with the role's parameters. Please make job searching more efficient and productive for all of us by including salary or banding in your job listings. Thanks for coming to my TED Talk.

    2
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • BIY People & Talent // BIY Future Talent

    5,873 followers

    9mo
    • Report this post

    **WHAT DO YOU SAY ABOUT SALARY EXPECATIONS ON A JOB APPLICATION?**   This week, Silv gives you the headlines on sharing salary expectations when you’re applying for a new role. Usually it will depend on a number of different factors which might include your experience level, or the volume of projects you’ll be working on, or the scale of the projects for example. It’s important to be realistic, go too high, and you might have priced yourself out, go too low and you’re not going to feel like you’re financially progressing. Maybe, in this instance, the role isn’t just about the financial progression? Remember, in some instances the draw to the role wont just about the financial progression. Maybe the broader opportunity it offers might be more important to you at that particular stage of your career? If you’re going through a recruiter, don’t forget, they’re available to talk these kind of things through with you, so get their thoughts and take on board their advice if it feels reasonable and realistic!   Little disclaimer from Silv: these are just my opinions, opinions I've formed over nearly 20 years of working in music, but still, purely my own thoughts, experiences and opinions. I'm sure there will be recruiters and hiring managers out there who have differing thoughts, experiences and opinions, or people that have done things a different way, and hurray for that!! Additional context is always required to give specific tailored advice to each individual, so this is just a starting point, something more generic which I hope is helpful. Remember, there are no fixed route or rules, everyone's path is their own, so if in doubt, just do you!   #musicbusinesscareers #musicbusinessjobs #BIYindustryinsights #salarytips #jobapplicationtips

    22 1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Ahmed Gamal
    8mo
    • Report this post

    It's really sad 🥺 Really sad how unfair some practices the selection process that recruiters use sometimes with candidates .... why I say that? Once I received a question iny inbox: "Whenever I applied for a job online, they ask me about my current salary and expected salary and it's really annoying giving the fact that maybe I am underpaid in my current job plus I cannot say an assumption about the expected salary given the fact that the job description in job posts is normally not that clear for you to suggest a number" I replied with this: Hey friend, here's what I used to do with that part of the application whenever I used to apply for a job whether it's full time/ part time / hybrid or else ... "With all due respect to your application process, I would like to highlight that requesting current salary does not provide a fair assessment of my true market value, Furthermore, asking for an expected salary before there is more clarity on job requirements and expectations is not not practical from my prospective" Many recruiters would be pissed when they see this, a few will respect it ... those are the ones you wanna do business with 👍

    2
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Joseph Casuccio
    9mo
    • Report this post

    In today’s world, job descriptions should be more than a vague list of responsibilities. Time is a finite resource, and job seekers deserve the same clarity employers expect. A few non-negotiables: 1️⃣ Salary ranges should be visible. Whether that means “$75K+” or “$100K–$115K,” it provides both sides with the necessary information. Job seekers know their worth. Let’s not waste time. 2️⃣ Assessments and additional steps should be stated upfront. If the process requires testing, case studies, or multiple rounds, clearly specify them. Let candidates decide if they want to invest. And give clear parameters for what “meeting requirements” looks like. Because honestly, it’s exhausting to pour hours into an application only to discover it suddenly requires my left kidney to get an interview. It’s 2025. The world is chaotic enough. Let’s make hiring more effective for everyone.

    2

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • CVyangu

    50 followers

    8mo
    • Report this post

    Have you ever wondered why you're not landing your dream global job, despite being highly skilled? The answer might be simpler than you think. As an African professional, you may be unknowingly underselling yourself. ⚡ Many of us are often too quick to accept the first salary offer thrown our way. We assume that negotiating would be viewed as greed or disrespect. But here's a secret: EVERY SALARY IS NEGOTIABLE. International companies expect you to haggle. In fact, many intentionally low-ball the initial offer, anticipating that you'll negotiate for a higher figure. Here's what you can do: 1. Research the average salary range for your desired role in the specific country or company of interest. 2. Understand your value. Know the worth of your skills and the experience you bring to the table. 3. Negotiate confidently but politely. Remember, you're not being greedy; you're asking for what you deserve. 🔥 Pro Tip: Use facts and figures during negotiation. Show them the market rate, and highlight your skills and experience that justify a higher pay. Remember, the worst they can say is no. But even then, you win. You earn their respect for standing up for your worth. And that alone sets you apart and makes you a strong candidate for future career advancement opportunities. Are you ready to start negotiating your worth? Visit cvyangu.com to learn more. #CareerDevelopment #AfricanProfessionals #JobSearch #CareerSuccess #CVyangu

    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Shivam Jagtap
    8mo
    • Report this post

    𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝘁𝗼 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗼 “𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁’𝘀 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗿𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘀𝗮𝗹𝗮𝗿𝘆?”⬇️ . . You can stay polite, protect your leverage, and keep things moving. Here’s a simple playbook you can use in UK/ Ireland (and most places where salary history is allowed). 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗰𝗵 (𝗾𝘂𝗶𝗰𝗸) Know your target range before the call. Keep it 10–15% wide and aim for the top end. Redirect from history → budget & expectations. If pressed, repeat calmly. If they still push, decide if you want to continue. 𝗣𝗼𝗹𝗶𝘁𝗲 𝘀𝗰𝗿𝗶𝗽𝘁𝘀 (𝗽𝗶𝗰𝗸 𝗼𝗻𝗲) 1) Ask for the band first “Happy to talk numbers. Could you share the budgeted range for this role? I can confirm if my expectations align.” 2) Market-based “I prefer to focus on market rate for the role. Based on my experience with [skills/scope], I’m looking at €X–€Y. How does that compare to your band?” 3) Last resort (still not revealing) “I can confirm I was competitively paid for my level. For this opportunity, I’m seeking €X–€Y.” 𝗤𝘂𝗶𝗰𝗸 𝗱𝗼/𝗱𝗼𝗻’𝘁 𝗗𝗼: Bring a clear range, ask for the band, include total comp (base + bonus/benefits). 𝗗𝗼𝗻’𝘁: Apologize, justify with personal finances, or share a number you’ll regret. ♻️Repost for someone who is preparing for interviews. Check helpful resources on my profile ▶️ Shivam Jagtap #SalaryNegotiation #PayTransparency #CareerAdvice #JobSearch #InterviewTips #Compensation #Hiring #Recruiting #IrelandJobs #DublinJobs #TechJobs #MarketingJobs

    27 2 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • CDR | Creative Digital Recruitment

    1,497 followers

    8mo
    • Report this post

    Still seeing “competitive salary” in job ads? Here’s what candidates actually hear: “We’ll decide what you’re worth later.” Transparency isn’t just good ethics, it’s good hiring. Jobs with salary ranges attract the right applications, faster hires and greater trust. #CreativeDigitalRecruitment #HiringBestPractice #PayTransparency #Recruitment https://lnkd.in/gkzUM-Px

    Kelly Kettle-White

    Creative, Digital, Media & Tech Hiring Partner ⬧ Helping Agencies & Brands Strengthen Teams ⬧ Founder of CDR

    8mo

    You wouldn’t buy a house, a car or go to a rental viewing without knowing the cost. So why are so many job ads still hiding salary details? This week alone, I’ve spoken to three people who went through full interview processes without knowing what the role actually paid. All talented. All experienced. All left wondering if it was even worth their time. When ads say “competitive salary” or “based on experience,” what candidates really hear is: “We’ll decide what you’re worth later.” It’s not about being money-driven, it’s about clarity, respect and efficiency. Jobs with salary ranges draw more applications, shorten the time-to-hire process, filter out mismatched expectations and build trust. Across Australia, pay transparency in job postings is increasing year-on-year because it is effective. And the best candidates rarely apply blind - they don’t have to. If you want stronger applicants and smoother hiring, start with the basics: Show what the job is worth. Because you wouldn’t buy anything without knowing the price and people shouldn’t have to apply for jobs without knowing the salary. (Unless it’s voluntary, of course.) #CreativeDigitalRecruitment #HiringTips #PayTransparency #Recruitment #HiringBestPractice

    2 1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

    • Report this post

    You wouldn’t buy a house, a car or go to a rental viewing without knowing the cost. So why are so many job ads still hiding salary details? This week alone, I’ve spoken to three people who went through full interview processes without knowing what the role actually paid. All talented. All experienced. All left wondering if it was even worth their time. When ads say “competitive salary” or “based on experience,” what candidates really hear is: “We’ll decide what you’re worth later.” It’s not about being money-driven, it’s about clarity, respect and efficiency. Jobs with salary ranges draw more applications, shorten the time-to-hire process, filter out mismatched expectations and build trust. Across Australia, pay transparency in job postings is increasing year-on-year because it is effective. And the best candidates rarely apply blind - they don’t have to. If you want stronger applicants and smoother hiring, start with the basics: Show what the job is worth. Because you wouldn’t buy anything without knowing the price and people shouldn’t have to apply for jobs without knowing the salary. (Unless it’s voluntary, of course.) #CreativeDigitalRecruitment #HiringTips #PayTransparency #Recruitment #HiringBestPractice

  • Md. Ebrahim Chowdhury Jewel
    9mo Edited
    • Report this post

    “A job circular without a clear salary is just a trap." 👨💼 HR posted a job without mentioning salary. 👨💻 Candidate applies, full of hope and effort. 👨💼 HR: "We are impressed with your skills. What are your salary expectations?" 👨💻 Candidate: "Sir, may I know the budget for this role first?" 👨💼 HR: "What’s your expectation?" 👨💻 Candidate: "50,000 BDT per month." 👨💼 HR: "Sorry, that’s beyond our range." 👨💻 Candidate leaves, frustrated, feeling undervalued. Message to the respected HR👨💼: Please mention the salary in the job posting! Not doing so wastes the time of both companies and candidates. Writing “Competitive Salary” or a huge, unrealistic range like 20,000–200,000 BDT only creates confusion.🙏 Message to the Candidate👨💻: Don’t feel small for asking the right questions. Your skills, time, and effort deserve respect. Never let vague postings or indecisive recruiters make you question your worth. "Please repost to spread awareness." Copied by “Md. Ibrahim Hussain” (Sir) is wall.
